segments.s537-g25: ببردند is a past plural verb 'they took/led' (3rd pl.). بالای + noun is a locative phrase 'on/above'; زرین is an epithet 'golden' modifying لگام 'bridle'. The whole chunk means 'they carried/led up the golden bridle'.
segments.s537-g26: نشست is the past singular of نشستن 'he sat/settled' (here the subject is the مهتر). از + برش functions poetically as a locative/ablative 'from his side' (i.e. beside him); برش is a compressed possessive/partitive 'his side/part'. مهتر is the chief/headman and شادکام is an adjectival epithet 'joyful/fortunate'.
segments.s537-g27: بفرمود is an honorific/past-form verb 'he commanded/said'. پس means 'then', شهریار is 'the king', and بلند is an epithet 'lofty/exalted' describing the king; word order follows elevated poetic style (verb before subject).
segments.s537-g28: زدن is the infinitive/verbal noun 'to set up/erect' (classical usage of زدن for planting/erecting poles). پیش دریا = 'before/in front of the sea'; دو دار بلند = 'two tall poles/masts' (دار 'tree/pole' used for mast). The phrase states the command's content: to plant two tall masts by the sea.
segments.s537-g29: دو بدخواه را is the definite object ('the two ill-wishers'); را is the object marker. زنده modifies them 'alive'. بر دار کرد literally 'did (put) on the gallows' — کرد here as causative/making done: 'he caused them to be placed on the gallows' (i.e. executed by hanging).
segments.s537-g30: دل دشمن is a possessive/genitive phrase 'the enemy's heart' (object/topic). از خواب is an ablative 'from sleep'; بیدار کرد is a transitive verbal phrase 'made (them) awake' or 'roused'. Here کرد is causative: 'he roused the enemy's heart from sleep.'
segments.s537-g31: بیامد is the past singular 'he came/advanced'. ز is the poetic form of 'from'; قلب سپه = 'the heart/core of the army/host' — together 'he came forth from the midst of the army'. شهرگیر is an appositive/epithet 'the besieger/conqueror (of cities)'.
segments.s537-g32: بکشت is an archaic past 'he killed' (classical Shahnameh usage). آن دو تن را = 'those two bodies/people' (را marks the definite object). به باران تیر is an instrumental phrase 'by/with a rain (shower) of arrows' — به used instrumentally and باران تیر is figurative 'a shower of arrows'.
segments.s537-g9: چنین (thus) is fronted for emphasis, followed by گفت (said) in the simple past third‑person. زان باره is a temporal/occasional phrase 'on that occasion' that qualifies the speech; شاه اردشیر is the subject (title + name) placed after the verb in the poetic inversion common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s537-g10: که here introduces the reported utterance ('that' / 'that is'), followed by نزدیک جنگ ('the battle is near' — نزدیک as predicate/adjective + جنگ). آی‌ای is a vocative/exclamation ('O!'), addressing شهرگیر (vocative noun 'besieger') at the end; the vocative follows the clause for forceful address.
segments.s537-g11: اگر starts a conditional ('if'). گم is an adjectival/predicative form 'lost', and شود is the present/subjunctive/auxiliary 'becomes/is' (3rd person) forming the protasis. از میان is the prepositional phrase 'from among', and هفتواد is the proper name/thing being spoken of.
segments.s537-g12: نماند is the negative present/future 'will not remain' (literally 'will be left'), followed by به چنگ تو 'in/into your hand/grasp' (به + چنگ + possessive تو). جز introduces the exception 'except', and رنج و باد lists what will remain — رنج 'suffering'; باد here is an archaic/exclamatory or optative particle conveying woe/ill (not literal 'wind'), a common poetic formula.
segments.s537-g13: که here functions as 'for/because'. من is the subject 'I'. کرم را is the definite object 'generosity/kindness' marked by را, and دادم is the past 'I gave'. ارزیز گرم are attributive adjectives ('precious' / 'worthy' and 'fervent'/'warm') describing the gift or its effect; in poetry adjectives can follow the verb cluster they modify.
segments.s537-g14: شد is the past 'became'. آن دولت 'that/state/fortune' is the subject which becomes something. و links the clauses; رفتن is a verbal noun/infinitive 'going/departure', modified by تیز 'swift' and نرم 'soft/gentle' — poetically saying the swift motion was softened (compound predicate after شد applies to the subject).
segments.s537-g15: شنید is the simple past 'heard'. آن همه لشکر 'all that host/army' is the subject (آن + همه + لشکر). آواز شاه 'the king's voice' is the direct object phrase heard — in Persian the object can follow the subject in poetry for rhythm.
segments.s537-g16: به سر بر نهادند is a compound verbal phrase: به سر 'on (their) heads' + بر نهادند (past 3rd plural of نهادن) 'they placed/put on'. ز آهن کلاه is a genitive/ablative phrase 'helmets of iron' (ز = 'of/from' in poetic register, here 'made of'). Word order is typical: object/adjunct (material) after the verb cluster for metrical reasons.
segments.s537-g17: ازان = archaic/poetic form of از آن, an adverbial 'from that/thereupon' placed before the phrase; دل گرفتند is an experiential expression: literally 'their hearts were seized' (دل گرفتن = to be grieved/afflicted). ایرانیان at the end is the (implicit) subject/agent ('the Iranians') — in this construction گِرفتند is used in the past to describe a state that befell them.
segments.s537-g18: ببستند is past plural 'they bound/tied' (epic form with the extra initial b-); با درد is an instrumental/attendant phrase 'with sorrow/grief'; کین را is a definite/marked object ('the hatred') — را marks کین as the object being bound; میان functions adverbially 'between' (i.e. 'between them' is understood). The subject 'they' is the previously named ایرانیان.
segments.s537-g19: سوی + لشکر کرم = 'toward the host/army of generosity' where کرم ('generosity') is an epithet modifying لشکر. برگشت is 'turned/returned'. باد here is the classical optative particle (sense 'may') rather than the literal 'wind', so برگشت باد is a wish/optative: 'may (it) turn'. Word order (verb + باد) is typical in older poetic Persian for optative wishes.
segments.s537-g20: گرفتار شد is a passive/resultative: 'became trapped/was caught'. در میان هفتواد is a locative phrase 'in the midst of Haftvād' (هفتواد is a proper name). The clause lacks an explicit subject here in the segment and so reads impersonally or carries the subject from the narrative.
segments.s537-g21: همان نیز = 'that same (one) also' (همان pointing back to a previously mentioned person). شاهوی is an adjective/epithet 'kingly/royal'; عیار here means 'bravo/valiant fellow' (archaic/epic sense). اوی is a third‑person genitive/relativizing clitic meaning 'his/of him' (so 'the royal bravo of his'). The whole phrase predicates an identity of the referent.
segments.s537-g22: که introduces a relative clause 'who/which'; مهتر پسر بود = 'he was the elder/leading son' (مهتر = elder/chief, followed by the noun پسر). و links an additional predicate: سالار اوی = 'and his commander' (سالار = commander, اوی = 'his'). بود is the past copula 'was'. The clause defines the bravo's relation to the addressee.
segments.s537-g23: فرود آمد is a past intransitive verb 'he descended/alighted (arrived)'. از باره = 'on the occasion/errand of' (باره = occasion/mission) and شاه اردشیر names the patron 'King Ardashir'; the prepositional phrase specifies purpose or occasion: 'on King Ardashir's business'.
segments.s537-g24: پیاده = 'on foot' (he dismounted/was on foot). ببد is an archaic past copula/verb form (variant of بود/بُد) meaning 'became/was'; here it links the state 'on foot' to the predicate that follows. پیش او شهرگیر = 'before him (in his presence) a city‑taker / besieger' (شهرگیر = besieger). Together: 'on foot he became/stood before him as a besieger.'
segments.s537-g49: وزان (= از آن) is a fused prepositional adverb 'from that (place/time)' and begins the clause; جایگه is the noun 'place' (object of 'از'). شد is the past/preterite of شدن used here in the intransitive sense 'turned/was turned' (i.e. 'went/turned toward'); سوی is the preposition 'toward'; طیسفون is the place-name Ctesiphon. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic rhythm: 'From that place he turned toward Ctesiphon.'
segments.s537-g50: سر بخت is a poetic genitival phrase ('head/leader of fortune' i.e. fate itself); بخت = 'fate/fortune'. بدخواه functions as an adjective/agentive 'ill‑wishing, hostile' (fate is described as hostile). کرده is the perfective/past participial sense 'has made/caused' and links to نگون, which is a predicative adjective meaning 'overthrown, brought low'. The clause therefore says that hostile fate caused him to be cast down.
segments.s537-g51: چنین است is a copular phrase 'it is thus/so'; رسم is the substantive 'custom/way' and جهان جهان is a poetic reduplication of 'جهان' (the world) for emphasis. Ordered poetically (چنین است + subject) rather than the ordinary prose order 'رسم جهان چنین است'.
segments.s537-g52: همی here means 'always/still' and modifies the verb phrase. راز خویش is 'its own secret' — in the manuscript the possessive is split (خوی + ش), so خوی + ش together form the reflexive/possessive 'own/of itself'. از تو دارد نهان is an inverted order meaning 'keeps hidden from you' (دارد = 'has/keeps', نهان = 'hidden', از تو = 'from you').
segments.s537-g53: نسازد is the negative/impersonal use of ساختن meaning 'it will not be made/it does not suit' (i.e. 'if it cannot be arranged'). تو is the addressed 'you'. ناچار is an adverb/adjective 'compelled/necessarily' modifying the following imperative. با او بساز is an imperative 'make (peace) with him / put up with him' (بساز = 2nd person imperative of ساختن used here as 'come to terms').
segments.s537-g54: که introduces the explanatory clause 'for/that' (giving the reason). روزی here is adverbial 'at one time / one day' (i.e. 'sometimes/at one time') used with نشیب 'descent/low point' and است 'is'; و links the clauses and the second روزی ... فراز states the counterpart 'one day is ascent/height'. The line states the alternation of fortunes.
segments.s537-g55: چو = 'when' (temporal conjunction). از گفته کرم is a prepositional-phrase 'from/after the telling/speech of generosity' (گفته = 'what was said/account', کرم = 'generosity'). پرداختم is the first-person past of پرداختن used in the sense 'I finished/ I turned away (from) / I completed' — here 'I finished treating the topic.'
segments.s537-g56: دری دیگر is 'another (dari) piece/tale' — دری here names a unit/style (poetic usage) and دیگر 'other/another'. از اردشیر marks the source 'from Ardashir'. آختم is an archaic/poetic first‑person past form (here used to mean 'I took up/treated/recited'); the literal dictionary senses are limited, but in context it functions as 'I began/turned to (another story)'.
segments.s537-g41: سپرد is a simple past 3rd‑person verb 'he entrusted/handed over'. آن زمان is an adverbial phrase 'at that time' placed after the verb (Persian allows flexible order). کشور و تاج و تخت is a coordinated direct object: 'the realm, the crown and the throne' (تاج و تخت is a standard pairing meaning sovereignty/royal power).
segments.s537-g42: بدان here functions as a dative 'to those' (historical contraction of به + آن). میزبانان is the plural noun 'hosts/custodians'; بیدار is an attributive adjective 'wakeful/alert'; بخت is a noun 'fate/fortune' used as an epithet — together: 'to those watchful hosts of fate'.
segments.s537-g43: وزان is a conjunctive/adverbial 'and/from that' linking to the previous event. جایگه 'place/post' is the locative or figurative spot referred to; رفت is past 'he went' (3rd pers.); پیروز و شاد are predicate adjectives 'victorious and glad', describing his condition as he left.
segments.s537-g44: بگسترد is a past/preterite literary form 'he spread/extended'. بر کشور پارس is the postpositional phrase 'over the country of Pars' (بر = 'upon', کشور = 'realm', پارس = 'Pars/Persia'). داد at the end is a noun 'justice' (داد), so the clause means 'he spread justice over Pars'.
segments.s537-g45: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آسوده‌تر is the comparative adjective 'more at ease/relieved'. گشت is the past intransitive 'became'. مرد و ستور ('men and beasts') are the subjects that 'became more at ease'.
segments.s537-g46: بیاورد is a past-tense verb 'he brought' (literary form). لشکر is the direct object 'an army/host'. سوی is the preposition 'toward', and شهر گور 'the city of Gūr' is the destination (here گور is a place name rather than its common noun meanings).
segments.s537-g47: به کرمان is the prepositional phrase 'to Kerman'. فرستاد is the past verb 'he sent/dispatched'. چندی سپاه means 'some troops' or 'a number of soldiers' (چندی as a quantifier preceding سپاه).
segments.s537-g48: یکی is an indefinite 'one/a certain'. مرد شایسته = 'a man worthy' (شایسته as attributive adjective). تاج و گاه is a compact epic formula meaning 'the crown and the (royal) seat/occasion' — i.e. kingship or authority; the two nouns are paired to indicate suitability for rule.
segments.s537-g1: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آگاه شد is a light/adjectival predication formed with the adjective آگاه ('aware') plus the past auxiliary شد ('became') = 'became aware / learned'. زان سخن is a poetic prepositional phrase ('of that speech') where زان = 'of/from that' modifies سخن. The proper name هفتواد is the subject and comes at the end of the clause (typical flexible, somewhat archaic word order).
segments.s537-g2: دلش = دل + enclitic pronoun ش 'his' (his heart). گشت is past 'became/turned' (synonymous here with شد). پردرد is an adjectival compound 'full of pain / sorrowful' describing دل. و links the two coordinative predicates. سر پر ز باد is a poetic image: سر 'head', پر 'full (of)', ز = poetic 'of/from', باد 'wind' — literally 'head full of wind', i.e. agitated or distracted thought; word order is head + adjective phrase.
segments.s537-g3: بیامد که is a purposive/introducing clause 'he came so that... / he came to...'. دژ را is the definite object ('the fortress') marked by را. کند here is the present/subjunctive form of کردن ('to do / to make'); together with خواستار ('desirous' / 'seeking') it expresses purpose: 'he came seeking the fortress' (object before the verb/predicate, a common older/topical word order).
segments.s537-g4: بران باره is a fixed poetic phrase 'on that occasion / at that time' (بران = 'thereon, upon that' + باره = 'occasion'). بر شد here means 'rose up / came upon' or simply 'rose/occurred'. دمان شهریار is literal 'the king's dāman (hem/edge)', a poetic expression for the king's person or moving/standing (here best read as 'the king rose' — the image is physical: the king's hem/edge rose = the king stood up).
segments.s537-g5: بکوشید is a past tense verb 'he tried / strove'. چندی here means 'for a while' (duration). نیامدش سود is a negative impersonal/result clause: نیامد = 'did not come', ش = enclitic 'to him', سود = 'benefit' → 'no benefit came to him' (i.e., it did him no good). Word order places the negated verb before its object and enclitic.
segments.s537-g6: که introduces an explanatory clause 'because'. بر باره دژ is a locative/against-phrase 'around/against the fortress' (بر + باره used poetically). پی شیر بود states existence: پی can mean 'after / in the vicinity of / in pursuit of' and here functions to place the lion in relation to the fortress; شیر = 'lion'; بود is the past copula 'was' (there was a lion). Taken together: 'because there was a lion by/at the fortress.'
segments.s537-g7: وزان = 'and/from that' (a linking, slightly archaic/poetic connector). روی لشکر بیامد literally 'the army came in appearance' — read as 'the army arrived' (روی here is part of the phrase indicating appearance/coming). چو + کوه is a simile 'like a mountain' (چو = 'as/like', کوه = 'mountain'), so the army's arrival is compared to a mountain (i.e., massive).
segments.s537-g8: بماندند is past 'they remained / were left'. با + داغ و درد = 'with grief and pain' (داغ = burning grief; درد = sorrow/pain). آن گروه 'that group / that troop' is the subject of بماندند: 'that band remained in grief and sorrow.' Word order places the verb before the prepositional and nominal complement (poetic flexibility).
segments.s537-g33: به تاراج داد is a light-verb phrase: به + noun (تاراج) + داد (gave). به here marks the beneficiary/goal ('to/for') of the giving (sense 0), تاراج is the noun 'pillage' (sense 0), and داد is the past verb 'gave' (sense 1). آن همه خواسته is a noun phrase 'all those desired things' with آن as the demonstrative 'the/that' (sense 1), همه 'all' (sense 0) and خواسته used as a noun 'desired/claimed things' (sense 1) that is the thing being given to plunder.
segments.s537-g34: شد begins a resultant-state clause 'became/was made' (sense 0). از marks source 'from' (sense 0) and is followed by خواسته 'the spoils/desired things' (sense 1) as the material/source. لشکر is 'army' (sense 0) and آراسته is an adjective 'adorned/embellished' (sense 0); the construction means roughly 'the army was adorned/furnished from the spoils.'
segments.s537-g35: به دژ is a simple directional phrase 'to the fortress' with به = 'to' (sense 0) and دژ = 'fortress/citadel' (sense 0). هرچ بود is a relative/indefinite clause 'whatever was' where هرچ = 'whatever' (sense 0) and بود is the past copula 'was' (sense 1). از کران تا کران is an adverbial 'from edge to edge' with از = 'from' (sense 0), کران = 'edge/limit' (sense 0), and تا = 'as far as / even' used here to mark the other limit (sense 2).
segments.s537-g36: فرود آوریدند is a compound verb meaning 'they brought down' or 'they carried down/into' formed from the noun فرود 'descent' (sense 0) + the past plural آوریدند 'they brought' (sense 0). فرمانبران (sense 0, 'the obedient/submissive') follows the verb and functions as the group brought down (object) — classical poetic word order often places the affected persons after the verb.
segments.s537-g37: ز پرمایه is a prepositional phrase 'from the rich/substantial (ground)' with ز as the poetic form of 'from' (sense 0) and پرمایه as an adjective 'substantial, rich' (sense 0). چیزی 'something' (sense 0) is the head noun, and که introduces a relative clause ('that/which', sense 0). بد دلپذیر is a compound adjective where بد 'bad' (sense 0) negates or qualifies دلپذیر 'pleasant' (sense 0), producing 'ill-pleasing' or 'disagreeable.'
segments.s537-g38: همی is a preverbal particle giving iterative/continuous aspect ('still/kept on', sense 0) and precedes the past verb تاخت 'rushed/galloped/attacked' (sense 0). تا is the temporal/goal preposition 'until' (sense 0). خره اردشیر is a nominal phrase 'Ardashir's clod/chunk' by placing the possessor after the noun (classical Persian poetic style), with خره = 'clod' (sense 0) and اردشیر the proper name (sense 0).
segments.s537-g39: بکرد is an archaic/perfective past 'he did/caused' formed with the past stem (sense 0). اندران is a locative/adverbial 'in that/among them/therein' (sense 1) and governs کشور 'country/land' (sense 0), so اندران کشور = 'in that country'. آتشکده 'fire-temple' (sense 0) is the direct object of the verb, meaning 'he caused/established a fire-temple there.'
segments.s537-g40: بدو 'to it/unto it' (sense 0) refers back to the fire-temple. تازه شد is an adjectival verbal phrase 'became new/was renewed' with تازه 'new/fresh' (sense 0) and شد 'became' (sense 0). مهرگان و سده are the coordinated festival names receiving that renewal: مهرگان (Mehregan, sense 0) and سده (Sadeh, here the festival sense 1).

sentences.s537-s13: They led up the golden bridle, and the chief sat beside him, joyful.
sentences.s537-s14: Then the lofty king commanded (and) to erect two tall masts before the sea.
sentences.s537-s15: He had two ill-wishers put alive upon the gallows; he awakened the enemy's heart from sleep.
sentences.s537-s16: He came forth from the heart of the host, and with a shower of arrows he killed those two men.
sentences.s537-s5: Thus spoke Ardashir the king on that occasion: 'O besieger, the battle is near!'
sentences.s537-s6: If Haftvād is lost from among them, nothing will remain in your grasp except suffering and (woe).
sentences.s537-s7: For I gave away generosity; that fortune grew warm, and swift departing became gentle.
sentences.s537-s8: All the army heard the king's voice; they put iron helmets upon their heads.
sentences.s537-s9: Thereupon the Iranians' hearts were seized; with grievous pain they bound hatred between them.
sentences.s537-s10: May the wind turn toward the generous host; (one) became trapped in the midst of Haftvād.
sentences.s537-s11: He, too, was his royal bravo — who was his elder son and his commander.
sentences.s537-s12: He descended on King Ardashir's errand; on foot he became (stood) before him a besieger.
sentences.s537-s25: From that place he went toward Ctesiphon; ill fortune, hostile to him, brought him low.
sentences.s537-s26: Such is the way of the world; the world always keeps its own secret hidden from you.
sentences.s537-s27: If it will not come about for you, you must perforce reconcile with him; for one day is descent and another day is ascent.
sentences.s537-s28: When I had finished speaking of generosity, I took up another tale from Ardashir.
sentences.s537-s21: At that time he entrusted the realm, the crown and the throne to those watchful hosts of fate.
sentences.s537-s22: From that post he went forth victorious and joyful, and spread justice over the land of Pars.
sentences.s537-s23: When men and beasts became more at ease, he brought an army toward the city of Gūr.
sentences.s537-s24: He sent some troops to Kerman; (and) a certain man worthy of the crown and the seat.
sentences.s537-s1: When Haftvād learned that speech, his heart became sorrowful and his head was filled with wind.
sentences.s537-s2: He came to demand the fortress; on that occasion the king rose (to his feet).
sentences.s537-s3: He strove for a while, but it did him no good, because around the fortress there was a lion.
sentences.s537-s4: From that time the army came like a mountain, and that band remained with grief and pain.
sentences.s537-s17: He handed all those things over to pillage; from the spoils the army was made splendidly outfitted.
sentences.s537-s18: They carried into the fortress whatever existed from edge to edge; the submissive were brought down.
sentences.s537-s19: From the fertile ground something unpleasant arose; it rushed on until it reached Ardashir's clod.
sentences.s537-s20: He established a fire-temple in that land; to it Mehregan and Sadeh were newly given (i.e., newly instituted there).
